refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Compare against ≤1% threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on control fidelity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission protocols tuned to network conditions to recover lost packets.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: use FEC for latency-sensitive control channels and QoS-based prioritization for telemetry, with monitoring and operator alerts.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining adaptive forward error correction (FEC) for control channels, QoS-based traffic prioritization for telemetry, real-time packet loss monitoring, and operator alerting. This balances latency constraints (critical for remote control) with reliability, and provides visibility into network health.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation control logic (Teleoperation module) with network infrastructure (Network Link interface) and safety alerting (SafetyAlert interface). This is acceptable because teleoperation inherently depends on network quality and safety feedback.
DP-D also couples with Tasking interface (operator commands) and Telemetry interface (vehicle state feedback), which is expected and necessary.
No hidden coupling to unrelated domains (e.g., vehicle propulsion, hydraulics) detected.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is intentional and localized to communication and safety domains. Recommend clear interface contracts to minimize ripple effects if network conditions or safety policies change.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment. Risk: If real-world conditions differ significantly, packet loss may exceed 1% despite DP-D implementation.
Assumption: FEC overhead (increased bandwidth) is acceptable within network capacity. Risk: If network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase effective loss.
Assumption: Operator can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts. Risk: If alerts are too frequent or unclear, operator may ignore them or become fatigued.
Assumption: QoS prioritization is supported by network infrastructure (routers, switches). Risk: If deployed on best-effort networks, QoS may not be enforceable.
Risk: High safety criticality means packet loss during critical maneuvers could endanger personnel or equipment. Mitigation required at safety services layer.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC algorithm and overhead budget, (2) QoS priority levels and traffic classes, (3) packet loss threshold for operator alert (e.g., >0.5% triggers warning), (4) fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds 1% (e.g., autonomous hold, operator takeover prompt). Coordinate with Safety Services to define safety-critical response.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the implementation of teleoperation communication quality assurance, including FEC encoding/decoding, QoS traffic management, packet loss monitoring, and operator alerting. It interfaces with the Network Link to transmit/receive packets and with Safety Services to escalate safety-critical packet loss events.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (e.g., move forward, rotate, stop) ingress to Teleoperation module. Teleoperation applies FEC and QoS prioritization before transmission over Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings) egresses from Teleoperation module to operator. Teleoperation monitors packet loss on this feedback path and reports metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits encoded/prioritized packets and receives acknowledgments or retransmission requests. Network Link provides raw packet transport; Teleoperation adds reliability layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: If packet loss exceeds safety threshold (e.g., >1% sustained), Teleoperation escalates alert to Safety Services for operator notification and potential autonomous safeguard activation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural home for packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, QoS, and monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert escalation crosses boundary (acceptable and necessary).

Safety Services owns safety policy and response, but not the technical implementation of FEC/QoS. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would scatter communication quality logic across modules and violate separation of concerns.
Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and sensors, not communication infrastructure. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would create inappropriate coupling and diffusion.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could logically own packet loss quality, delegating to Teleoperation (the subsystem responsible for remote operation) is more cohesive and maintainable.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it already owns the teleoperation control logic and operator interface, (2) FEC and QoS are communication-layer concerns intrinsic to remote operation, (3) it has direct access to Tasking and Telemetry interfaces, (4) it can monitor packet loss in real-time and escalate to Safety Services without diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (FEC codec, QoS scheduler, monitoring, alerting), it may violate single-responsibility principle. Mitigation: Consider sub-modules (FEC codec, QoS engine, monitor) within Teleoperation boundary if complexity grows.

Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into packet loss (e.g., if Network Link only reports success/failure, not loss rate). Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface contract includes packet loss metrics or raw packet counters.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation may be delayed if Teleoperation is slow to detect packet loss. Mitigation: Implement low-latency monitoring (e.g., per-packet or per-burst loss detection) and define alert thresholds clearly.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the Network Link interface contract to specify: (1) packet loss metrics available to Teleoperation (e.g., loss rate, burst loss), (2) latency of loss detection, (3) QoS support (priority queues, traffic shaping). If Network Link does not provide sufficient metrics, extend it or add a monitoring sub-interface. Define SafetyAlert escalation protocol (e.g., threshold, message format, response time).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid FEC + QoS + monitoring + alerting) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The DP is specific, implementable, and measurable. FEC and QoS are proven techniques for packet loss mitigation. Monitoring and alerting provide visibility and safety feedback. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Minimal diffusion: DP-D is contained within Teleoperation module (95% containment score). Only SafetyAlert escalation crosses boundary, which is intentional and necessary for safety.
No diffusion to Vehicle, NGHE Solution, or other unrelated modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the correct architectural home for DP-D. It owns teleoperation logic, has access to Tasking and Telemetry interfaces, and can interface with Network Link and Safety Services without scattering responsibility.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end under controlled network conditions. Acceptance criterion (≤1%) is quantifiable and testable. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics for measurement.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns packet loss mitigation. Safety Services owns safety response policy. Vehicle and Network Link provide supporting interfaces. Responsibilities are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D specification to include FEC algorithm selection, QoS priority mapping, packet loss alert threshold, and fallback behavior (e.g., autonomous hold if loss exceeds 1%).
Extend Network Link interface contract to specify packet loss metrics and QoS support.
Define SafetyAlert escalation protocol (threshold, message format, response time) in coordination with Safety Services.
Plan sub-module decomposition for Teleoperation if FEC codec, QoS engine, and monitoring become complex.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation). DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail FEC algorithm, QoS policy, monitoring thresholds, and safety response logic. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition will clarify implementation details and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (target: <100ms detection latency).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement forward error correction (FEC) for control channels and selective retransmission for telemetry to minimize effective packet loss (target: reduce 5% raw loss to <1% effective loss).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall prioritize control packets (Tasking) over telemetry (Telemetry) via QoS to protect operator responsiveness under congestion.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall alert the operator and escalate to Safety Services if packet loss exceeds 1% for >5 seconds, triggering autonomous safeguard (e.g., hold position).

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of deployment.
FEC overhead (increased bandwidth) is acceptable within network capacity.
QoS prioritization is supported by network infrastructure.
Operator can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts.
Network Link interface can provide packet loss metrics or raw packet counters.
Safety Services will define and enforce safety response policy for high packet loss events.
open_risks: Real-world network conditions may differ from agreed conditions, causing packet loss to exceed 1%.
FEC overhead may paradoxically increase effective loss if bandwidth is severely constrained.
Operator alert fatigue if packet loss alerts are too frequent.
QoS may not be enforceable on best-effort networks.
Packet loss during critical maneuvers could endanger personnel or equipment (high safety criticality).
Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into packet loss metrics.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification (packet loss metrics, QoS support, latency).
FEC algorithm selection and overhead analysis (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes).
QoS priority mapping and traffic class definition.
Packet loss alert threshold and operator response protocol.
Safety Services safety response policy for high packet loss events.
Acceptance test plan for packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions.
Operator training and alert design to prevent fatigue.
